articles.headline: The Captive African Restored to Liberty (Prince / Abduhl Rahhahman Ibrahima)
articles.summary: A letter from a gentleman of Natchez to a lady of Cincinnati, dated April 7, 1828, recounting how 'Prince' (Abduhl Rahhahman Ibrahima), an African-born man captured in battle nearly forty years earlier and held in slavery on the plantation of Thomas Foster of Adams County, Mississippi, was finally freed. The writer describes helping Prince compose a 1826 letter to his native country by way of the American consul-general (Capt. John Mullowny), the resulting negotiations, and Foster's agreement to give up Prince (aged about 66); it also relays efforts to raise the roughly two hundred dollars needed to purchase Prince's wife Isabella. A widely reprinted item concerning the celebrated 'Prince Among Slaves'; no Philadelphia names.

articles.headline: Notice of the Protecting Society of the City and County of Philadelphia for the Prevention of Kidnapping and Man-stealing
articles.author: John Allen
articles.summary: A notice from the Protecting Society of the city and county of Philadelphia for the preventing of Kidnapping and Man-stealing, auxiliary to the Abolition Society of Philadelphia, dated Philadelphia April 24, 1828. The Society, formed in 1827, informs the coloured community of its object of combating the kidnapping of free persons of colour long carried on 'by a set of unprincipled men,' and directs persons seeking help recovering kidnapped friends to apply, personally or by post-paid letter, to the Secretary, John Allen. A directly Philadelphia-relevant anti-kidnapping organization.

articles.headline: Philadelphian Benevolence (recovery of Philadelphia children kidnapped by the Johnson ring)
articles.summary: A news item reprinted from a Philadelphia paper reporting that High Constable Garrigues returned to Philadelphia after nearly three months away on his second journey to Mississippi and Louisiana in pursuit of coloured children carried off from Philadelphia in the summer of 1825. Despite great trouble, risk, and expense he secured the final discharge and safe return of two of the victims, Clem Coxe and E. Laurence, who were examined at the Police Office. Their account matched those of other returned children: all had been stolen by Joseph Johnson or his agents, gagged, tied, and transported to Johnson's house on the Delaware-Maryland line and treated as chattel. A key document of the Cannon-Johnson kidnapping ring ('reverse Underground Railroad') that preyed on free Black Philadelphians.

events.name: Protecting Society of Philadelphia for the Prevention of Kidnapping and Man-stealing (auxiliary to the Abolition Society), notice
events.description: Notice published by the Protecting Society of the city and county of Philadelphia for the prevention of kidnapping and man-stealing, auxiliary to the Philadelphia Abolition Society, formed in 1827, informing the coloured community of its work against the kidnapping of free persons of colour and inviting applications for assistance to its Secretary. Signed by John Allen, Secretary, Philadelphia, April 24, 1828.

events.name: Recovery of Philadelphia children kidnapped by the Johnson ring (High Constable Garrigues' Mississippi journey)
events.description: Report of Philadelphia High Constable Garrigues' return from his second journey to Mississippi and Louisiana in pursuit of free Black Philadelphia children kidnapped in the summer of 1825. He recovered and returned two victims, Clem Coxe and E. Laurence, who were examined at the Philadelphia Police Office. Their testimony established that they had been stolen by Joseph Johnson or his agents and carried to Johnson's house on the Delaware-Maryland line — part of the Cannon-Johnson kidnapping ring that trafficked free Black Philadelphians into Southern slavery.
